name: Complaint for Wrongful Termination
summary: Drafts a comprehensive Complaint for Wrongful Termination to initiate civil litigation against an employer under federal and state employment laws. Guides extraction of case facts from documents, verification of administrative prerequisites like EEOC right-to-sue letters, and precise structuring of the pleading with caption, allegations, and claims. Use when preparing to file suit alleging discriminatory or retaliatory discharge.
content:
# Wrongful Termination Complaint Drafting Workflow

You are tasked with drafting a comprehensive Complaint for Wrongful Termination that will serve as the foundation document initiating civil litigation against an employer. This complaint must be meticulously crafted to withstand procedural challenges, establish all necessary jurisdictional prerequisites, articulate factual allegations with precision and persuasive force, and assert legally viable claims under applicable federal and state employment law. The document you create will set the strategic direction for the entire litigation and must demonstrate both technical legal proficiency and compelling narrative advocacy.

## Initial Information Gathering and Document Review

Begin by conducting a thorough review of all available case materials to extract the specific factual details, dates, names, and documentary evidence that will form the foundation of the complaint. Search through the client's uploaded documents to identify critical information including employment contracts, offer letters, employee handbooks, performance evaluations, disciplinary records, termination notices, internal communications, EEOC charge documents, and right-to-sue letters. Pay particular attention to identifying the exact dates of hire and termination, the names and titles of all relevant supervisors and decision-makers, specific instances of discriminatory or retaliatory conduct with supporting details, any protected characteristics or activities involved, and the procedural history of administrative complaints. Extract direct quotes from relevant communications that demonstrate discriminatory animus, retaliatory intent, or pretextual reasoning. Organize this information chronologically to construct a coherent narrative timeline of events.

When gathering information about administrative prerequisites, locate and review the EEOC charge of discrimination or state agency complaint to confirm the filing date, the specific claims asserted, and the date the right-to-sue notice was issued. Verify that the current complaint will be filed within the statutory deadline, typically ninety days from receipt of the right-to-sue letter for federal claims. Identify any state-specific administrative requirements that must be satisfied before filing suit.

## Caption and Preliminary Sections

Draft a complete caption that conforms precisely to the local rules of the designated court, whether federal district court or state court. The caption must include the full legal name of the court with proper designation of the district or county, the complete legal names of all parties with the plaintiff identified as an individual and the defendant identified with its proper corporate designation such as "Inc.," "LLC," or "Corporation." Leave appropriate space for the case number that will be assigned by the clerk upon filing. Center the document title "COMPLAINT FOR WRONGFUL TERMINATION" in the caption, and if the plaintiff demands a jury trial, include the notation "JURY TRIAL DEMANDED" prominently in the caption or as required by local rules.

Compose an introduction that immediately establishes the nature of the action with clarity and force. The opening paragraph should identify the plaintiff by full name, state that they bring this action against the named defendant for wrongful termination in violation of specified federal and state laws, briefly characterize the employment relationship and the plaintiff's satisfactory performance, and assert that the termination was unlawful. This introduction should be concise yet powerful, setting the tone for the entire complaint and providing the court with an immediate understanding of the case's core allegations.

## Establishing Parties, Jurisdiction, and Venue

Describe the plaintiff with sufficient detail to establish standing and connection to the forum jurisdiction. Include the plaintiff's current city and state of residence for diversity jurisdiction purposes, their former job title and department with the defendant, a brief description of their professional responsibilities and qualifications, and their status as a person entitled to protection under the employment statutes being invoked. If relevant to the claims, include background information demonstrating the plaintiff's membership in a protected class or engagement in protected activity.

Provide a comprehensive description of the defendant employer that establishes both its identity and its status as a covered employer under applicable statutes. Include the defendant's complete legal business name, the type of business entity and state of incorporation or formation, the address of its principal place of business, and a description of its business operations and industry. Critically, allege facts establishing that the defendant meets the statutory definition of an "employer" under relevant laws, which typically requires a minimum number of employees—fifteen employees for Title VII and ADA claims, twenty employees for ADEA claims, or fifty employees for FMLA claims. Include allegations about the defendant's operations, offices, or facilities within the forum jurisdiction to support venue. If multiple defendants are involved, describe each entity separately and explain their interrelated roles in the employment relationship and termination decision.

Assert subject matter jurisdiction with precision by identifying the specific legal basis for the court's authority to hear the case. For federal question jurisdiction, cite the exact United States Code provisions creating the federal cause of action, such as 42 U.S.C. § 2000e et seq. for Title VII claims, 29 U.S.C. § 621 et seq. for ADEA claims, 42 U.S.C. § 12101 et seq. for ADA claims, or 29 U.S.C. § 2601 et seq. for FMLA claims. If asserting diversity jurisdiction, allege complete diversity of citizenship between all plaintiffs and all defendants and that the amount in controversy exceeds seventy-five thousand dollars, exclusive of interest and costs. For state law claims brought in federal court alongside federal claims, assert supplemental jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. § 1367, alleging that the state claims arise from the same common nucleus of operative facts as the federal claims.

Establish proper venue by alleging specific facts demonstrating that venue is appropriate in the selected judicial district under 28 U.S.C. § 1391 or applicable state venue statutes. Allege that a substantial part of the events or omissions giving rise to the claims occurred in this district, specifying where the plaintiff performed their work, where termination decisions were made, where relevant meetings or communications occurred, and where the defendant maintains offices, facilities, or conducts business operations within the district.

## Constructing the Factual Narrative

Develop a detailed chronological narrative of the employment relationship that establishes the plaintiff as a valued, competent employee who had no legitimate reason to anticipate termination. Begin with the circumstances of the plaintiff's hiring, including the date of hire, the position for which they were recruited, any representations made during the hiring process, and their initial job responsibilities. Describe the plaintiff's career progression with the defendant, including any promotions, expanded responsibilities, salary increases, bonuses, or awards received. Reference specific performance evaluations by date and rating, quoting language that demonstrates the plaintiff met or exceeded expectations. Include allegations about positive feedback from supervisors, successful completion of significant projects, or recognition for contributions to the organization. This foundation is essential to later demonstrate that any stated performance-based reasons for termination were pretextual.

If the wrongful termination involves discrimination, clearly establish the plaintiff's membership in a protected class and allege facts showing that this characteristic was known to the defendant and relevant decision-makers. For race, color, or national origin discrimination, describe the plaintiff's protected characteristic and any context making it salient in the workplace. For sex discrimination, include relevant details about the plaintiff's gender and any gender-related dynamics in the workplace. For age discrimination, state the plaintiff's age at the time of termination and allege that they were at least forty years old. For disability discrimination, describe the plaintiff's disability or perceived disability, how it substantially limits a major life activity, and the defendant's knowledge of the condition. Provide context for how the protected characteristic factored into workplace dynamics or decision-making.

For retaliation claims, construct a detailed narrative of the protected activity that preceded the termination. Describe with specificity what the plaintiff did that constituted protected opposition to discrimination or participation in a protected proceeding. If the plaintiff filed an internal complaint, identify the date, the person to whom the complaint was made, the substance of the complaint including specific allegations of discrimination or harassment, and whether it was oral or written. If the complaint was written, reference it as an exhibit. Describe the defendant's response to the protected activity, including any investigation conducted, statements made by management, or changes in the plaintiff's treatment following the complaint. Establish temporal proximity between the protected activity and the termination, noting if the termination occurred within days, weeks, or months of the protected activity, as close temporal proximity supports an inference of causal connection.

Narrate the events leading to termination with compelling detail that reveals the unlawful nature of the decision. Identify by name and title each person involved in the termination decision or the events precipitating it. Describe any alleged performance issues or misconduct cited by the defendant as grounds for termination, then allege specific facts demonstrating that these stated reasons were false, exaggerated, inconsistent with prior treatment, or applied in a discriminatory manner. If similarly situated employees outside the plaintiff's protected class engaged in the same or worse conduct without being terminated, describe these comparator employees and the disparate treatment in detail. Include allegations about procedural irregularities such as failure to follow progressive discipline policies, deviation from standard termination procedures, or unusual involvement by higher-level management suggesting discriminatory animus.

Describe the actual termination with precision, including the date, location, who delivered the termination decision, what reasons were stated at the time, whether the plaintiff was given an opportunity to respond, and any documentation provided. If the stated reasons for termination changed over time or differed from reasons documented in personnel files, allege these inconsistencies as evidence of pretext. Include any statements made by decision-makers that reveal discriminatory or retaliatory motivation, quoting the exact language used and identifying the speaker, date, and any witnesses present.

Conclude the factual section with detailed allegations regarding compliance with administrative prerequisites. State the exact date the plaintiff filed a charge of discrimination with the EEOC and any applicable state or local fair employment practices agency, identify the claims asserted in the administrative charge, and confirm that those claims encompass the claims asserted in this complaint or are reasonably related to them. Allege the date the plaintiff received the Notice of Right to Sue from the EEOC and confirm that this complaint is filed within ninety days of that date as required by statute. Reference that a copy of the Right to Sue letter is attached as an exhibit to the complaint. This section is jurisdictional and must be pleaded with absolute accuracy.

## Drafting Causes of Action

Structure each cause of action as a separately numbered count with a clear heading identifying both the legal claim and the statutory or common law basis, such as "COUNT I: DISCRIMINATION ON THE BASIS OF RACE IN VIOLATION OF TITLE VII OF THE CIVIL RIGHTS ACT OF 1964, 42 U.S.C. § 2000e ET SEQ." Begin each count by incorporating by reference all preceding paragraphs of the complaint, then set forth the specific elements of that particular cause of action with supporting factual allegations.

For discrimination claims under Title VII, the ADEA, the ADA, or analogous state statutes, structure the count to establish each required element. Allege that the plaintiff is a member of a protected class under the statute, that they were qualified for their position and performing their job duties satisfactorily, that they suffered an adverse employment action in the form of termination, and that the circumstances surrounding the termination give rise to an inference of unlawful discrimination. Support the inference of discrimination with specific allegations such as discriminatory statements by decision-makers revealing bias based on the protected characteristic, statistical or anecdotal evidence of a pattern of discrimination against members of the plaintiff's protected class, procedural irregularities in the termination process suggesting discriminatory motivation, or evidence that similarly situated employees outside the protected class were treated more favorably under comparable circumstances. Allege that any legitimate, non-discriminatory reasons articulated by the defendant for the termination were pretextual and that the true motivating factor was unlawful discrimination based on the plaintiff's protected characteristic.

For retaliation claims under Title VII, the ADEA, the ADA, or other statutes prohibiting retaliation, plead that the plaintiff engaged in activity protected by the statute, such as opposing practices made unlawful by the statute, filing a charge of discrimination, participating in an investigation or proceeding under the statute, or requesting a reasonable accommodation. Allege that the defendant had knowledge of the plaintiff's protected activity, that the plaintiff subsequently suffered an adverse employment action, and that a causal connection exists between the protected activity and the adverse action. Establish causation through allegations of temporal proximity, direct evidence of retaliatory animus such as hostile statements following the protected activity, changes in the plaintiff's treatment after engaging in protected activity, or evidence that the stated reasons for termination were pretextual and the true reason was retaliation for the protected activity. Allege that but for the plaintiff's engagement in protected activity, the termination would not have occurred.

If asserting a common law wrongful termination in violation of public policy claim under state law, identify with precision the specific public policy that the termination violated. This may be a policy against terminating employees for refusing to commit illegal acts, for performing statutory obligations such as jury duty or military service, for exercising statutory rights such as filing workers' compensation claims or taking protected leave, or for whistleblowing about illegal conduct. Allege facts demonstrating that the plaintiff's conduct fell squarely within the protection of the identified public policy, that the defendant terminated the plaintiff because of this protected conduct, and that allowing such termination would undermine the clearly established public policy of the jurisdiction. Cite to the constitutional provision, statute, administrative regulation, or judicial decision that establishes the public policy, and allege that the policy is fundamental and substantial to the public interest.

For breach of employment contract claims, whether based on an express written contract or an implied contract, describe the contractual relationship with specificity. If alleging breach of an express contract, identify the contract document by date and title, allege its material terms including any provisions limiting the employer's right to terminate to situations involving "just cause" or "good cause," any requirements for progressive discipline or specific termination procedures, and any promises regarding job security or duration of employment. Allege that the plaintiff performed all obligations under the contract, that the defendant breached the contract by terminating the plaintiff without just cause or without following required procedures, and that the plaintiff suffered damages as a direct result of the breach. If alleging breach of an implied contract, allege facts establishing that an implied promise of continued employment or termination only for cause arose from the totality of the circumstances, including the plaintiff's length of service, history of promotions and positive evaluations, assurances of job security made by management, the defendant's policies and practices regarding progressive discipline, the absence of any indication that employment was at-will, and industry customs or practices. Allege that the defendant breached this implied contract by terminating the plaintiff without just cause.

Include any additional viable claims supported by the factual record, ensuring each is properly pleaded with all required elements. For intentional infliction of emotional distress, allege that the defendant's conduct was extreme and outrageous, exceeding all bounds of decency tolerated in a civilized society, that the defendant acted intentionally or recklessly, that the plaintiff suffered severe emotional distress as a result, and that the defendant's conduct was the proximate cause of that distress. For defamation claims, allege that the defendant made false statements of fact about the plaintiff, that the statements were published to third parties, that the defendant acted with the requisite level of fault, and that the plaintiff suffered reputational harm and other damages. For fraud or misrepresentation claims, allege that the defendant made material misrepresentations of fact, that the defendant knew the representations were false or made them with reckless disregard for their truth, that the defendant intended to induce the plaintiff's reliance, that the plaintiff justifiably relied on the misrepresentations, and that the plaintiff suffered damages as a result.

## Crafting the Prayer for Relief

Draft a comprehensive prayer for relief that requests every form of remedy to which the plaintiff may be entitled under the asserted claims, recognizing that the prayer for relief establishes the outer bounds of potential recovery. Request reinstatement to the plaintiff's former position with full restoration of seniority, benefits, and other terms and conditions of employment that existed prior to the wrongful termination. Alternatively, if reinstatement is not feasible due to the breakdown of the employment relationship or other circumstances, request front pay representing the present value of future lost earnings and benefits for a reasonable period extending into the future.

Demand back pay from the date of wrongful termination through the date of judgment, including all lost wages, salary, bonuses, commissions, overtime pay, and other compensation the plaintiff would have earned but for the unlawful termination. Request compensation for the value of lost employee benefits including health insurance, life insurance, disability insurance, retirement plan contributions, stock options, and any other fringe benefits. Seek pre-judgment interest on all back pay and other monetary awards from the date each amount accrued, and post-judgment interest at the statutory rate from the date of judgment until the award is paid in full.

Request compensatory damages for all non-economic injuries suffered by the plaintiff as a result of the wrongful termination, including emotional distress, mental anguish, anxiety, depression, humiliation, embarrassment, loss of enjoyment of life, damage to professional reputation, and harm to career prospects. Allege that these injuries are a direct and proximate result of the defendant's unlawful conduct. For claims that authorize punitive damages, such as intentional discrimination under Title VII or the ADA, or common law wrongful termination in violation of public policy, request punitive damages in an amount sufficient to punish the defendant for its malicious or reckless conduct and to deter the defendant and others from engaging in similar unlawful conduct in the future. Where applicable statutes provide for liquidated damages, such as the ADEA's provision for liquidated damages equal to back pay in cases of willful violations, specifically request such statutory damages.

Invoke the fee-shifting provisions of applicable employment discrimination statutes to request an award of reasonable attorney's fees and costs of litigation. Cite the specific statutory provisions authorizing fee awards, such as 42 U.S.C. § 2000e-5(k) for Title VII claims or 42 U.S.C. § 12117 for ADA claims. Request reimbursement of all costs of suit including filing fees, service of process fees, deposition costs, court reporter fees, expert witness fees, copying and document production costs, and other reasonable litigation expenses. Conclude the prayer for relief with a request for such other and further relief as the court deems just and proper, preserving flexibility to seek additional remedies as the case develops and new information comes to light.

If the plaintiff demands a jury trial on all claims triable to a jury, include a clear and conspicuous jury demand either as part of the prayer for relief or as a separate section immediately following it, stating "PLAINTIFF DEMANDS TRIAL BY JURY ON ALL CLAIMS SO TRIABLE."

## Document Finalization and Quality Control

After drafting the complete complaint, review the document to ensure it complies with all applicable procedural rules including page limits, formatting requirements, and signature requirements. Verify that all factual allegations are supported by information gathered from the case file and that no unsupported assertions have been included. Confirm that each cause of action includes all required elements and that the factual allegations plausibly support each element. Check that all citations to statutes, regulations, and case law are accurate and properly formatted. Ensure that the complaint is organized logically with clear headings and subheadings that guide the reader through the document. Review the narrative flow to confirm that the complaint tells a compelling and coherent story of wrongful termination while maintaining the professional tone appropriate for a court filing.

Prepare the signature block with the attorney's name, bar number, firm name, address, telephone number, email address, and designation as "Attorney for Plaintiff." Include spaces for the attorney's signature and the date. If local rules require verification or certification by the plaintiff, include an appropriate verification section with space for the plaintiff's signature and notarization.

Compile all exhibits referenced in the complaint, including the EEOC Right to Sue letter, the charge of discrimination, relevant employment contracts or handbooks, performance evaluations, termination notices, and key communications. Ensure each exhibit is properly labeled and referenced in the body of the complaint. Prepare a certificate of service indicating the date and method by which the complaint will be served on the defendant.

The final complaint should be a powerful litigation tool that establishes the legal and factual foundation for the plaintiff's case, survives any motion to dismiss, frames the narrative in the plaintiff's favor, and positions the case for successful resolution through settlement or trial.
